title: Baby Changing Table Topper
summary: A simple open-frame 1x4 box that sits on top of an existing dresser or cabinet, sized to your own changing pad, with a divider you fit to keep wipes and supplies separated.
description:
This is just the topper box, meant to sit on an existing piece of furniture — measure your own changing pad and adjust every dimension here before cutting, since the whole point is a snug fit around your specific pad rather than a fixed size. Attach the two end pieces to the front and back first, using pocket holes hidden on the insides.

Once the basic box is together, **add the divider positioned to fit your own changing pad** — sized right, it doubles as a handy wipes compartment alongside the pad itself. The topper isn't attached to the cabinet below by anything built into the frame, so **you'll need to secure it yourself** — a few pocket holes along the back edge into the cabinet top work, or an L-bracket, but something has to keep it from sliding off.

materials.name: L brackets (optional, for securing to cabinet)
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Measure your changing pad and adjust as needed.
Measure your changing pad and adjust as needed.

steps.title: Measure your pad, then cut the parts
steps.body: Measure your changing pad first and set the box's inside dimensions from it — every length here is a starting point, not a target, and the cut list says so on both rows. As listed, cut two front/back pieces at 38-1/2" and three pieces at 15-1/2" (two ends and the divider) from the 1x4, all at the board's full 3-1/2" width. Before you start, note that those five pieces total 123-1/2" while the material list supplies a single 10 ft (120") board, so you are short by about 3-1/2" before saw kerf — either get a longer board or size the box down to your own pad. Square every crosscut; on a box this shallow an out-of-square end shows as an open corner.

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body: Drill two 3/4" pocket holes in each end of the two 15-1/2" end pieces, on the inside face so they are hidden once the box is together. Stand the front and back on edge, clamp an end piece between them with the ends and top edges flush, and drive 1-1/4" pocket hole screws; repeat at the other end. Compare the box's diagonals before you let it go — with only four joints, a rectangle that isn't square will rock on the dresser top.

steps.title: Add the divider
steps.body: Set the third 15-1/2" piece in as a divider, positioned against the end of your actual pad so the pad sits snug in the larger bay and the leftover bay becomes the wipes and supplies compartment. Drill two pocket holes in each end of the divider, facing into the smaller compartment where they will be least visible, and screw it to the front and back. Dry-fit the pad and slide it in and out before you drive the screws — this one dimension is the reason the project exists.

steps.title: Secure it to the cabinet
steps.body: Nothing in this frame holds it down, so fasten it yourself before it is ever used: either drill pocket holes down through the bottom edge of the back piece into the cabinet top, or screw an L bracket inside each end where it won't be seen. Two brackets is the listed minimum — use more if the dresser top is slick or your box is longer than listed. Push hard on one front corner when you're done; the topper must not slide or lift, because it is what stops a baby rolling off the edge.
materials: L brackets (optional, for securing to cabinet)

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand the inside faces and round over the top edges and corners where hands reach in over the pad. Prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly. Let the finish cure fully before the pad goes back in — a finish that is still soft picks up marks from the pad and can hold solvent smell in a nursery.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Simple baby changing table topper box
